Question.text: What are the weakest points in the DiscussionRoom synthesis quality?
How should the belief graph handle duplicate hypotheses across sessions?
What is the optimal rounds-per-query tradeoff for DiscussionRoom vs single-model LLM calls?
How should chitta-field integration surface past experiment failures to Hotr?
Which crates have the highest complexity and most potential for bugs?

Method.name: run: cargo clippy --manifest-path /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research/Cargo.toml --package cr-agents --package cr-llm --package cr-graph 2>&1 | head -40
run: cargo clippy --manifest-path /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research/Cargo.toml 2>&1 | grep 'warning\|error' | grep -v '^warning: unused' | head -20

Question.text: Does the current Synthesizer prompt reliably produce structured JSON output?
Is 2 rounds of debate sufficient or should it be configurable per agent?

Hypothesis.statement: DiscussionRoom synthesis loses minority viewpoints when participant count exceeds 3, because the aggregation step uses majority-vote-style merging rather than explicit conflict tracking

steps: run: grep -r 'synthesis\|aggregate\|merge\|combine' crates/cr-llm/src/ --include='*.rs' -n > /tmp/synthesis_sites.txt
Read synthesis_sites.txt to identify the merge algorithm
Construct 5 test rooms: 1 participant, 2, 3, 4, 5 — each with one injected contrarian-but-correct claim
run: cargo test --manifest-path Cargo.toml -p cr-llm -- synthesis 2>&1 | tee /tmp/synthesis_test.log
Score each output: does the contrarian claim appear verbatim, paraphrased, or disappear entirely
Plot survival rate vs participant count; fit logistic regression

Hypothesis.statement: Synthesis prompt templates instruct the LLM to 'summarize discussion' rather than 'resolve disagreements', causing the synthesizer to produce vague consensus statements that omit actionable conclusions

steps: run: grep -r 'synthesis_prompt\|SYNTHESIS\|synthesize\|final_answer' crates/ --include='*.rs' -rn > /tmp/prompts.txt
Extract the literal prompt strings from the source
Collect 20 recent DiscussionRoom transcripts from cresearch-*.log files

run: python3 -c "import re, json, sys; [print(json.dumps({'len': len(re.findall(r'\\b(should|must|recommend|action|implement)\\b', open(f).read(), re.I))})) for f in sys.argv[1:]]" cresearch-self*.log
Score each synthesis output: count imperative/actionable tokens vs hedging tokens (ratio as specificity metric)
Rewrite synthesis prompt to explicitly request 'list disagreements, resolve each, output concrete next steps'
Re-run same 20 transcripts with new prompt, compare specificity scores with Wilcoxon signed-rank test

Hypothesis.statement: Cross-participant context truncation degrades synthesis quality in long rooms: later participants receive truncated earlier turns and produce locally coherent but globally inconsistent contributions that the synthesizer cannot reconcile

steps: run: grep -r 'max_tokens\|context_len\|truncat' crates/ --include='*.rs' -n > /tmp/context_budget.txt
Identify per-participant token allocation in the room dispatch logic
run: awk '/PARTICIPANT|tokens_used|context/ {print NR": "$0}' cresearch-self*.log > /tmp/token_trace.txt
Parse token_trace.txt to extract (participant_index, tokens_available, tokens_used) triples
run: Rscript -e "d <- read.csv('/tmp/token_trace.csv'); cor.test(d$participant_index, d$tokens_available, method='spearman')"
For rooms where participant N>=3 had <25% of participant 1's token budget, manually rate synthesis coherence 1-5
Fit linear model: coherence ~ min_token_ratio; p<0.05 confirms hypothesis

Observation.summary: Function/method 'Read synthesis_sites.txt to identify the merge algorithm' not found

$ cargo test --manifest-path Cargo.toml -p cr-llm -- synthesis 2>&1 | tee /tmp/synthesis_test.log
error: failed to parse lock file at: /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research/Cargo.lock

Caused by:
  lock file version `4` was found, but this version of Cargo does not understand this lock file, perhaps Cargo needs to be updated?
No symbols found for query: Collect 20 recent DiscussionRoom transcripts from cresearch-*.log files

Hypothesis.statement: Semantic deduplication using cosine similarity ≥0.85 on hypothesis embeddings reduces redundant belief nodes by >40% without losing distinct hypotheses, as measured on a synthetic corpus of 200 hypothesis pairs with known duplicate/distinct labels

steps: run: python3 scripts/gen_hypothesis_pairs.py --n-duplicates 80 --n-near 60 --n-distinct 60 --out data/hyp_pairs.jsonl
run: cargo run --release --manifest-path chitta-field/Cargo.toml --bin embed_pairs -- --input data/hyp_pairs.jsonl --out data/hyp_embeddings.npy
run: python3 scripts/sweep_cosine_threshold.py --embeddings data/hyp_embeddings.npy --labels data/hyp_pairs.jsonl --thresholds 0.75,0.80,0.85,0.90,0.95 --out results/dedup_threshold_sweep.csv
Inspect precision/recall curve; select threshold maximizing F1 while keeping recall ≥0.95 for distinct hypotheses
Report: reduction rate at optimal threshold vs. baseline (no dedup)

Hypothesis.statement: Merging duplicate hypotheses by averaging their evidence scores (rather than keeping max or first-seen) produces better-calibrated confidence estimates, measurable via Brier score on held-out experiment outcomes

steps: run: python3 scripts/extract_dup_hypotheses.py --sessions cresearch-self*.log --similarity-threshold 0.85 --out data/dup_groups.jsonl
run: python3 scripts/simulate_merge_strategies.py --dup-groups data/dup_groups.jsonl --strategies max,first,mean --outcomes data/experiment_outcomes.jsonl --out results/merge_calibration.csv
run: Rscript scripts/calibration_analysis.R --input results/merge_calibration.csv --out results/brier_scores.png
Accept mean-merge if its Brier score is ≥5% lower than both alternatives; otherwise keep max as conservative default

Hypothesis.statement: Cross-session duplicate detection with a persistent bloom filter indexed by hypothesis embedding quantiles is faster than full pairwise cosine search (O(1) vs O(n)) while maintaining <2% false-negative rate at n=10,000 hypotheses

steps: Implement BloomHypothesisIndex in chitta-field/src/bloom_dedup.rs using 4-bit quantized embedding hashes as keys, capacity=20000, error_rate=0.01
run: cargo test --manifest-path chitta-field/Cargo.toml bloom_dedup -- --nocapture
run: cargo bench --manifest-path chitta-field/Cargo.toml --bench dedup_bench -- --sample-size 50 2>&1 | tee results/bloom_bench.txt
run: python3 scripts/plot_dedup_scaling.py --bench results/bloom_bench.txt --out results/dedup_scaling.png
Accept bloom approach if p99 lookup <1ms at n=10,000 and false-negative rate <2% on the hyp_pairs.jsonl benchmark corpus

Hypothesis.statement: DiscussionRoom with 2 rounds achieves 90%+ of the quality ceiling of 5+ rounds while reducing token cost by 60%, making 2 rounds the optimal tradeoff for code review and debugging tasks

steps: Curate 50 representative SE tasks from chitta-research logs: 10 bug diagnoses, 10 code reviews, 10 architecture decisions, 10 refactors, 10 test generation tasks
Define a quality rubric: correctness (0-3), completeness (0-3), actionability (0-2), novelty-over-single-model (0-2) — total 10 points, scored by blind LLM judge (opus-4.6)
run: python3 .scripts/benchmark_discussion_rounds.py --tasks tasks.jsonl --rounds 1 2 3 5 --baseline single --model claude-sonnet-4-6 --output results/rounds_tradeoff.jsonl
run: Rscript .scripts/analyze_rounds_tradeoff.R results/rounds_tradeoff.jsonl --plot results/quality_vs_cost_curve.png
Fit a diminishing-returns curve (quality ~ a*(1 - exp(-b*rounds))) and find the knee point; compare token cost at knee vs. single-model baseline

Hypothesis.statement: Task complexity is the dominant moderator of round-count benefit: simple tasks (single-file bugs, syntax errors) show no quality gain beyond 1 round, while complex tasks (multi-module architecture, cross-cutting refactors) require 3+ rounds to surpass single-model quality

steps: Label each benchmark task with complexity score: LOC of relevant context, number of distinct modules, dependency depth (from AST/LSP data)
run: python3 .scripts/complexity_scorer.py --tasks tasks.jsonl --repo /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research --output tasks_with_complexity.jsonl
Run DiscussionRoom at rounds={1,2,3,5} for each task, recording per-task quality delta vs. single-model
run: Rscript .scripts/complexity_moderation.R tasks_with_complexity.jsonl results/rounds_tradeoff.jsonl --output results/moderation_analysis.png

Fit interaction model: quality_delta ~ rounds * complexity_bin (low/medium/high); identify the complexity threshold above which 3+ rounds yield statistically significant improvement (p<0.05, Wilcoxon)

Hypothesis.statement: A dynamic routing policy — 1 round for tasks with entropy(initial_response) < threshold, 3 rounds otherwise — matches fixed 3-round quality while consuming 40% fewer tokens on average

steps: Define response entropy as token-probability entropy of the first model's answer (use logprobs) or, if unavailable, semantic disagreement between two independent single-model draws measured by 1 - cosine_similarity(embed(r1), embed(r2))
run: python3 .scripts/entropy_early_exit.py --tasks tasks_with_complexity.jsonl --threshold-search 0.1 0.2 0.3 0.4 --max-rounds 5 --output results/entropy_routing.jsonl
For each threshold candidate, compute (mean_quality, mean_tokens_used) across all 50 tasks; plot Pareto frontier against fixed-round baselines
run: Rscript .scripts/pareto_analysis.R results/entropy_routing.jsonl results/rounds_tradeoff.jsonl --output results/pareto_frontier.png
Select threshold maximizing quality/token ratio; validate on a held-out 20-task set; report 95% CI on token savings and quality delta vs. fixed-3-round baseline using bootstrap resampling

Hypothesis.statement: Storing experiment failures as negated causal triplets (hypothesis→FAILED_WITH→error_pattern) in chitta-field and querying them at Hotr planning time will reduce duplicate failed hypotheses by ≥40% compared to flat text recall of failure notes.

steps: run: grep -E 'hypothesis|experiment|failed' cresearch-self*.log | python3 .scripts/extract_hypothesis_outcomes.py > /tmp/hypothesis_outcomes.tsv
Audit chitta for existing failure memories: call mcp__chitta__recall with query='experiment failed error' and tag filter evidence_type=failure
Define triplet schema: (hypothesis_id, FAILED_WITH, failure_mode) and (failure_mode, ROOT_CAUSE, cause_tag); store 10 representative past failures via mcp__chitta__remember with these triplets
run: python3 .scripts/simulate_hotr_recall.py --strategy keyword --cycles 25 --output /tmp/keyword_recall_dedup.json
run: python3 .scripts/simulate_hotr_recall.py --strategy triplet --cycles 25 --output /tmp/triplet_recall_dedup.json
run: Rscript .scripts/compare_dedup_rates.R /tmp/keyword_recall_dedup.json /tmp/triplet_recall_dedup.json --alpha 0.05

Hypothesis.statement: Injecting a compact failure digest (top-5 failures by semantic similarity to current research question) into Hotr's context window via smart_context, rather than relying on passive recall, will reduce wasted experiment cycles by surfacing actionable failure signals that passive recall misses due to embedding drift.

steps: Extend chitta MCP schema to support evidence_type filter in smart_context; verify via mcp__chitta__get_evidence_type that 'failure' type is registered
run: grep -c 'FAILED\|error\|aborted' cresearch-self*.log | awk -F: '{sum+=$2} END{print "total failures:", sum}'
Tag all existing failure-outcome memories with set_evidence_type=failure using mcp__chitta__set_evidence_type in a batch script: run: python3 .scripts/tag_failure_memories.py
Implement active-injection wrapper in skills/yajña/hotr_dispatch.sh: call chitta smart_context, prepend top-5 failure digest to Hotr's system prompt
Run 15 passive turns and 15 active-injection turns with same research question seed; record which hypotheses Hotr selects
run: python3 .scripts/measure_failure_overlap.py --passive-log /tmp/passive_turns.json --active-log /tmp/active_turns.json --metric jaccard,success_rate

Hypothesis.statement: A UCB1-weighted failure memory recall (using mcp__chitta__recall_ucb1 with failure memories receiving a penalty bonus) will outperform recency-sorted recall for preventing Hotr from re-entering known failure basins, because failure relevance decays slower than general research relevance.

steps: Verify UCB1 recall respects priority_tier: call mcp__chitta__recall_ucb1 and inspect whether tier=critical memories rank higher
Set a sample of 20 known failure memories to priority_tier=critical via mcp__chitta__set_priority_tier; leave 20 equivalent failure memories at default tier
run: cargo run --release --manifest-path chitta-field/Cargo.toml --example ucb1_priority_sim -- --failure-boost 2.0 --trials 20 --output /tmp/ucb1_boosted.json
run: cargo run --release --manifest-path chitta-field/Cargo.toml --example ucb1_priority_sim -- --failure-boost 1.0 --trials 20 --output /tmp/ucb1_baseline.json
run: Rscript .scripts/basin_reentry_test.R /tmp/ucb1_boosted.json /tmp/ucb1_baseline.json --metric reentry_rate,recall_precision --test wilcoxon

Hypothesis.statement: The chitta-field Rust crate has the highest cyclomatic complexity per module due to its concurrent data structures (HNSW, BM25, OpLog) and unsafe memory-mapped I/O, making it the primary source of latency bugs and race conditions.

steps: run: cargo install cargo-geiger tokei 2>/dev/null; cd /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research && for crate in chitta-field chitta chitta-mcp; do echo "=== $crate ==="; tokei $crate/src --output json 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); [print(k, v['stats']['complexity'] if 'complexity' in v.get('stats',{}) else 'N/A') for k,v in d.items()]"; done
run: cd /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research && cargo geiger 2>&1 | grep -E '(crate|unsafe)' | head -60

run: cd /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research && git log --oneline --all -- 'chitta-field/**' | grep -iE '(fix|bug|race|deadlock|panic|overflow)' | wc -l && git log --oneline --all -- 'chitta/**' | grep -iE '(fix|bug|race|deadlock|panic|overflow)' | wc -l
run: cd /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research/chitta-field && grep -rn 'unsafe' src/ | wc -l && grep -rn 'Arc\|Mutex\|RwLock\|AtomicU' src/ | wc -l

Hypothesis.statement: The RPC handler layer (chitta/include/chitta/rpc/field_handler.hpp) has disproportionate complexity relative to its line count because it multiplexes 175+ tools with ad-hoc dispatch logic, leading to unhandled edge cases in error paths.

steps: run: pip install lizard -q && lizard /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research/chitta/include/chitta/rpc/field_handler.hpp --CCN 10 --length 50 2>&1 | head -80
run: cd /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research && grep -c 'case\|"method"\|dispatch\|handle_' chitta/include/chitta/rpc/field_handler.hpp

run: cd /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research && grep -n 'TODO\|FIXME\|HACK\|XXX\|WARN\|assert' chitta/include/chitta/rpc/field_handler.hpp chitta/src/simple_cli.cpp 2>/dev/null | wc -l
run: cd /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research && git log --oneline -- 'chitta/include/chitta/rpc/' | grep -iE '(fix|crash|null|undefined|error)' | head -20

Hypothesis.statement: The VakYantra ONNX embedder (chitta/include/chitta/vak.hpp) is the highest-risk component for silent correctness bugs because floating-point truncation in embedding normalization is never validated, causing subtle semantic drift in recall quality over time.

steps: run: grep -n 'norm\|sqrt\|dot\|float\|f32\|f64\|epsilon\|assert' /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research/chitta/include/chitta/vak.hpp | head -40
run: grep -n 'norm\|normalize\|dot_product\|cosine' /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research/chitta-field/src/*.rs 2>/dev/null | head -40

run: cd /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research && python3 -c "
import subprocess, json
# embed two near-identical strings and check cosine similarity via chitta CLI
pairs = [('memory retrieval', 'recall from memory'), ('bug fix', 'software defect correction')]
for a, b in pairs:
    print(f'pair: {a!r} vs {b!r}')
"
run: cd /maps/projects/fernandezguerra/apps/repos/chitta-research && grep -rn 'f32\|f64' chitta-field/src/store.rs | grep -E '(cast|as f|into\(\))' | head -20
